title: What is normalization?
summary: Three standard forms of Codd: 1NF (the key), 2NF (the whole key), 3NF (nothing but the key). Include denormalisation.
content_html:
What is normalization?

String of rules to ensure relational data model quality. Each normal form must be achieved in this order: 1NF → 2NF → 3NF.

"Every non-key attribute must provide a fact about the key, the whole key, and nothing but the key" — William Kent

Primary Key & Foreign Key

Primary Key (PK): uniquely identifies a row. There are 2 types: Natural Key (actual data) and Surrogate Key (artificial, eg auto-increment ID).

Foreign Key (FK): refers to the PK of the related table. Implement a relationship between 2 tables.

1NF – "The Key"

Every non-key field must provide facts about the key. There are no multivalue fields or repeating groups. All rows must have the same number of fields.

For example: If a Tour has many Keywords → create a separate TourKeywords table.

2NF – "The Whole Key"

Applicable when PK is a composite key. Every non-key field must depend on the entire PK (not just a part).

For example: LineItems(InvoiceNo, ProductName, Quantity, Manufacturer) — Manufacturer depends only on ProductName → separated from the Products table.

3NF – "Nothing But the Key"

Every non-key field depends only on the key field, not on other non-key fields. Eliminate transitive dependencies.

For example: If DifficultyDescription depends on DifficultyRating (non-key) → separate TourDifficulty table.

Denormalisation

Reverse normalization when needed: (1) Improve performance, (2) Save historical data (eg: Invoice.Address copied from Customer.Address to save the address at the time of purchase).
